claims.text: About 25 percent of people consider themselves superstitious — but the actual rate is probably higher.

evidence.quote:
d
out why
[Music]
about 25 percent of people consider
themselves superstitious but it's
probably more than that do you say bl

claims.rationale: Gallup polls (2007, 2017) and similar surveys put self-reported superstitiousness in the 20-30% range in the US, consistent with the 25% claim. The 'probably more' qualifier is reasonable since social-desirability bias likely depresses self-report.

claims.text: One theory is that Pope Gregory, who became pope after his predecessor died from the plague, started saying 'god bless you' whenever someone sneezed as a way of wishing good health.

evidence.quote:
l another theory is that pope
gregory who became pope after his
predecessor died from the plague would
say god bless you whenever someo

claims.rationale: Pope Gregory I (the Great) became pope in 590 AD during the Plague of Justinian's recurrence in Rome. Folklore attributes the 'bless you' practice to his pontificate, but no contemporary papal records confirm he originated it — the etymology is plausible but unverifiable. One of several competing folk theories — no directTruth because the attribution is not historically documentable.

claims.text: Eighty percent of high-rise buildings in the U.S. don't have a 13th floor, and Lufthansa and Air France don't have a 13th row — fear of the number 13 is called triskaidekaphobia.

evidence.quote:
ilding check out
the elevator eighty percent of high-rise
buildings in the u.s don't have a 13th
floor lufthansa and air france don'

claims.rationale: Otis Elevator estimates ~85% of US high-rises skip the 13th-floor button, consistent with '80%'. Lufthansa does skip row 13 (and 17 for Italian/Brazilian passengers); Air France does skip row 13. Specific percentages vary by source. Broadly accurate.

dependencies.rationale: Concrete example illustrating widespread superstitious behavior in modern infrastructure.

claims.text: In much of East and Southeast Asia, tetraphobia (fear of the number 4) is widespread because the Chinese word for four sounds almost identical to the word for 'die'.

evidence.quote:
nd southeast asia you'll find
tetraphobia fear of the number four in
the chinese language and its subgroups
the word for and the word die

claims.rationale: Mandarin '四' (sì, four) and '死' (sǐ, die) are near-homophones — well-documented linguistic basis for the East Asian taboo. Many Chinese hospitals, hotels, and apartment complexes skip floor 4. Textbook fact in cross-cultural psychology.

dependencies.rationale: Cross-cultural example showing superstition is universal not just Western.

claims.text: The word 'sinister' comes from the Latin word 'sinistra' (left), reflecting the ancient Roman belief that everything on the left side was evil.

evidence.quote:
il or sinister
in fact the word sinister comes from the
latin word sinistra so waking up on the
left side of the bed was lite

claims.rationale: Etymology is correct: 'sinister' (English) ← 'sinister' (Latin) meaning 'on the left side'; the pejorative connotation 'unlucky/evil' developed in late Latin from Roman augury practice (omens on the left were inauspicious). Standard etymology.

claims.text: Superstitions are essentially a coping mechanism for anxiety — a way to feel control over an unpredictable world.

evidence.quote:
re the short answer
is it's a coping mechanism for anxiety
the world is a crazy unpredictable place
it's only natural that we want to h

claims.rationale: Standard psychological account, well-supported by Skinner's superstition-in-pigeons paradigm and Malinowski's anxiety-uncertainty theory. The ritual-as-anxiety-management mechanism is mainstream in social psychology.

claims.text: A study reported by the Association for Psychological Science showed that people who crossed their fingers for luck had increased manual dexterity, memory, and even improved golf scores.

evidence.quote:
ve a study
reported by the association for
psychological science showed that people
who cross their fingers for luck had an
increase

claims.rationale: Damisch et al. 2010 (Psychological Science) reported lucky-charm and finger-crossing effects on golf-putting accuracy and memory tasks. The findings have been challenged by failed replication attempts (Calin-Jageman & Caldwell 2014). The host's framing as a fact is overconfident given the replication crisis context — no directTruth because the underlying study has not robustly replicated.

dependencies.rationale: Empirical evidence for the anxiety-coping mechanism thesis.

claims.text: At a well in Northumberland England dedicated to the goddess Coventina, archaeologists found 16,000 coins from different eras of the Roman Empire.

evidence.quote:
ss of wells and springs coventina
sixteen thousand coins from different
eras of the roman empire were found
there
but we don't have a fou

claims.rationale: Coventina's Well at Carrawburgh on Hadrian's Wall is a real archaeological site. Excavations (1876 and onward) recovered approximately 13,000-16,000 coins per various counts, consistent with the host's number. Roman votive deposit context confirmed.

dependencies.rationale: Ancient evidence for the coin-throwing-as-anxiety-relief tradition.

claims.text: Alexander the Great believed in a river that cured old age, but he was only 32 when he was poisoned to death.

evidence.quote:
nds of years alexander the great
believed in a river that cured old age
he was only 32 when he was poisoned to
death so who knows if he

claims.rationale: Alexander died at age 32 in 323 BC — confirmed. Cause of death is contested by historians: poison theory exists but is not consensus (malaria, typhoid fever, alcohol-related liver failure are more commonly cited). The 'river that cured old age' myth appears in the Romance of Alexander tradition (Pseudo-Callisthenes), legendary not historical. No directTruth because the poison cause is contested.

claims.text: Legend says Ponce de Leon accidentally discovered Florida while searching for the Fountain of Youth, but most historians think that's an exaggeration.

evidence.quote:
nce de leon accidentally
discovered florida when he was searching
for the fountain of youth now most
historians think that's an exagger

claims.rationale: Modern scholarship (J. Michael Francis, Jerald Milanich) has found no contemporary 16th-century source linking Ponce de León's 1513 Florida voyage to a Fountain of Youth quest. The story originates in Herrera's later (1601) chronicle and was likely embellishment. Host's 'most historians think it's an exaggeration' is accurate.

claims.text: Houdini campaigned against fake spiritualism in front of Congress, calling mediums who claim to communicate with the dead 'a fraud from start to finish'.

evidence.quote:

magician and escape artist houdini
himself made it a mission to expose
these frauds houdini took it so
seriously that he campaigne

ess this thing they call
spiritualism we're in a medium
intercommunicates with the dead is a
fraud from start to finish superstiti

claims.rationale: Houdini's 1926 testimony before the House Subcommittee on Foreign Affairs is real and well-documented. He campaigned for legislation against fortune-telling fraud and exposed mediums (Margery Crandon case) using his stage-magic expertise. The quote is authentic.

dependencies.rationale: Specific historical case of debunking superstition-based fraud.

claims.text: Relief pitcher Turk Wendell never touched the foul lines, jumped over them, kept four pieces of black licorice in his mouth on the mound, and brushed his teeth between innings.

evidence.quote:
than relief pitcher turk wendell he made
sure to never touch the foul lines he
would actually jump over them and each
time he took the

claims.rationale: Turk Wendell's superstitions are MLB legend — Cubs/Mets reliever 1993-2004. The licorice-and-toothbrushing routine is documented in his ESPN profiles and SI features. Foul-line jumping is well-attested. Core facts correct.

dependencies.rationale: Vivid example of high-performance use of superstition for anxiety control.

claims.text: Superstition isn't OCD — psychologists don't see them as connected; people with OCD can't NOT do their rituals because failing to causes great anxiety.

evidence.quote:
ed
his teeth sounds like ocd it does but
psychologists don't see them as
connected and people with ocd can't not
do the rituals they do

claims.rationale: Clinically correct distinction. OCD is defined by ego-dystonic compulsions (DSM-5), while superstitious behavior is typically ego-syntonic and non-impairing. The compulsion-vs-habit distinction is fundamental to OCD diagnostic criteria.

claims.text: Controlling anxiety is healthy, so practicing harmless superstitions like throwing salt over your shoulder may help save your own well-being.

evidence.quote:
t to quell your anxiety and
controlling anxiety is healthy so by all
means throw salt over your shoulder
knock on wood and say bless yo

claims.rationale: Well-aligned with mainstream psychology — anxiety regulation through ritual has real cognitive-behavioral benefits (reducing rumination, increasing perceived control). Standard advice in low-stakes contexts. Host's framing is reasonable.

dependencies.rationale: Practical recommendation built on the anxiety-coping framework.
Damisch finger-crossing study cited as evidence ritual provides real benefit.

claims.text: Superstitions are universal across human cultures and history.

evidence.quote:
thank you so superstitions are universal
we've all had those days where from the
moment we wake up we're grumpy annoyed
at the whole world

claims.rationale: Cross-cultural anthropology robustly supports the universal-superstition claim — Atran, Boyer, and others document supernatural-belief systems in every documented culture. The cognitive-default-to-agency-detection finding makes universality the expected outcome.

dependencies.rationale: Specific example (tetraphobia) supporting the universality thesis.
